sections.heading: Forward Chaining
sections.body: Forward chaining starts from known facts and repeatedly applies rules whose conditions are satisfied, generating new facts, until the desired conclusion is derived (or no more rules apply). It's data-driven — reasoning forward from what's known toward whatever it implies.

sections.heading: Backward Chaining
sections.body: Backward chaining starts from a goal (the thing to be proven) and works backward, checking which rules could conclude that goal and recursively trying to satisfy their conditions. It's goal-driven — often more efficient than forward chaining when you have a specific question to answer rather than wanting to derive everything possible.

sections.heading: Resolution
sections.body: Resolution (introduced with propositional logic) is also a core inference technique in first order logic reasoning systems — repeatedly combining clauses to derive new ones, commonly used together with proof by contradiction to establish that a statement must be true.

sections.heading: Proof by Contradiction
sections.body: Proof by contradiction (also called refutation) works by assuming the negation of what you want to prove, then showing that assumption leads to a logical contradiction with known facts — since the assumption can't be true without contradiction, the original statement must be true.

quiz.question: Forward chaining reasons:
options: Backward from the goal to known facts
Forward from known facts, applying rules until a conclusion is reached

quiz.question: Backward chaining is often more efficient than forward chaining when:
options: You want to derive every possible fact
You have a specific goal/question to answer
There are no rules at all

quiz.question: Proof by contradiction works by:
options: Assuming the negation of the goal and showing it leads to a contradiction
Only applying to predicate logic, never propositional logic
